tech-ai.title: Google and FBI warn of ransomware group that sends fake IT workers to hack victims in person
tech-ai.summary: The FBI and Google have issued a warning about a cybercriminal gang called the Silent Ransom Group, which uses a scary new tactic: sending people who pretend to be IT support workers to law firms and other businesses. Once inside, these fake employees steal data using USB drives or gain remote access to computers. Ransomware is malicious software that criminals use to lock up important files and demand money to unlock them. This is significant because it shows how cybercriminals are getting more creative and personal in their attacks, moving beyond just hacking from computers to actually showing up in person. If you work anywhere, understanding this threat helps you spot suspicious behavior and protect your workplace's information.

tech-ai.title: Former cyber executive turned whistleblower accuses IBM of covering up several data breaches
tech-ai.summary: A former cybersecurity executive at IBM has become a whistleblower—someone who exposes wrongdoing at their company—and is suing IBM for allegedly covering up major data breaches that happened in the mid-2010s. A data breach occurs when hackers gain unauthorized access to sensitive information like passwords, personal details, or company secrets. IBM is one of the world's largest technology companies, making this accusation especially serious because it affects millions of people who trusted IBM with their data. The lawsuit claims IBM didn't just fail to tell people about the breaches—they actively tried to hide them. This matters because it shows how important transparency is: companies need to be honest when they mess up so people can protect themselves.

tech-ai.title: Google will pay SpaceX $920M per month for compute
tech-ai.summary: Google has announced a massive deal where it will pay SpaceX—Elon Musk's rocket and satellite company—$920 million every single month for computing power. Computing power (or 'compute') is essentially renting access to powerful computers that run AI models and process data. Google made this deal because demand for its new AI products has been unexpectedly high, meaning more people want to use these AI tools than Google anticipated. This partnership shows how AI development requires enormous resources and how major tech companies are willing to pay huge amounts for the infrastructure needed to support it. For teenagers, this illustrates how AI isn't cheap—it requires massive investments in technology and partnerships between the world's biggest companies to actually work at scale.

tech-ai.title: Reid Hoffman is leaving Microsoft's board to go 'founder mode' with startup Manus
tech-ai.summary: Reid Hoffman, a legendary tech entrepreneur who co-founded LinkedIn and has been on Microsoft's board for over a decade, is stepping down from his high-paying board position to focus on a startup called Manus. Manus is an AI company focused on drug discovery—using artificial intelligence to help scientists find and develop new medicines faster. Being on a major company's board is usually a prestigious, lucrative role that doesn't require daily work, so this decision shows how much Hoffman believes in Manus's potential. The move is significant because successful entrepreneurs leaving secure, well-paid positions to bet on startups signals confidence in new technology and innovation. This demonstrates how AI-powered healthcare and drug development is becoming exciting enough to attract top talent willing to take major career risks.

tech-ai.title: What happens when your phone is confiscated at the airport
tech-ai.summary: U.S. Customs and Border Protection agents have the power to confiscate—take away—your phone at airports, even if you're a U.S. citizen returning home and haven't done anything wrong. One Minnesota labor organizer had her phone taken and searched without a warrant (official court permission) when returning from Europe. A warrant is a legal document that typically requires a judge to approve before police can search your belongings. This practice raises serious privacy concerns because your phone contains extremely personal information: your messages, photos, location history, and private conversations. This matters to teenagers because it affects your digital rights and freedom—the ability to keep your private life actually private. Understanding these airport powers helps you know your legal rights and think critically about privacy in the digital age.

tech-ai.title: Supabase doubles valuation to $10B in 8 months
tech-ai.summary: Supabase, a software company built on open-source technology (code that anyone can view and modify), just doubled its valuation to $10 billion in just eight months. A valuation is what investors think a company is worth—hitting $10 billion makes Supabase a 'unicorn,' an extremely rare startup status. This rapid growth happened because Supabase benefits from AI coding tools like Claude and Codex, which help developers write software faster and better. Open-source projects becoming massively valuable companies shows how collaborative technology and AI are transforming entrepreneurship. For teenagers interested in coding or startups, this illustrates how learning to use modern AI development tools can create enormous opportunities and how open-source projects can become billion-dollar businesses.

tech-ai.title: GM's electric future depends on a new battery — and this building
tech-ai.summary: General Motors (GM), one of the world's largest automakers, is betting its electric vehicle future on a new battery technology that it plans to deploy a year earlier than originally scheduled. The company believes this new battery will allow them to significantly reduce EV prices, making electric cars affordable for more people. This specific manufacturing facility is crucial—it's where these batteries will be mass-produced. Electric vehicles are important for fighting climate change because they produce zero emissions, unlike gas-powered cars that pollute the air. GM's aggressive timeline shows how seriously traditional car companies are taking the shift to electric vehicles and how new battery technology is the key bottleneck. For teenagers, this matters because the cars you'll drive as adults will likely be electric, and breakthroughs like these determine whether they're affordable.

tech-ai.title: The most interesting startups right now want to get you off your phone
tech-ai.summary: While most tech startups are chasing AI investments worth billions, a countercurrent of entrepreneurs are building products designed to do the opposite: get people away from their screens. Examples include Board, a startup creating in-person social games, and cyberdeck creators building whimsical DIY computers designed to encourage people to go outside ('touch grass' is internet slang for logging off). This trend isn't just nostalgic backlash—it reflects genuine concern about screen addiction and loneliness in the digital age. These startups show that tech innovation isn't always about doing more online; sometimes it's about reconnecting with real-world experiences and human connection. For teenagers, this matters because it validates concerns many feel about phone addiction while showing that the tech industry itself is recognizing the problem and investing in solutions.

tech-ai.title: Founders share VC horror stories, and some are naming names
tech-ai.summary: A viral conversation exploded on X (formerly Twitter) this week where startup founders shared disturbing stories about their experiences with venture capitalists—investors who provide funding to early-stage companies. VCs wield enormous power because startups desperately need their money to grow, which sometimes allows investors to behave unprofessionally or unethically. Some of the stories that went viral are bizarre, while others describe genuinely harmful or infuriating behavior. This public conversation matters because it shines light on power imbalances in the tech industry that usually stay hidden behind closed doors. For teenagers interested in business or startups, understanding these dynamics helps you think critically about investment, workplace culture, and how money influences power in entrepreneurship.

tech-ai.title: As VC-backed e-bike startups went bankrupt, bootstrapped Lectric grew
tech-ai.summary: While many e-bike (electric bicycle) startups that received venture capital funding went bankrupt, Lectric—a company that bootstrapped (funded itself without outside investors)—thrived and recently launched three new brands in six months. Bootstrapping means entrepreneurs use their own money and reinvest profits rather than taking investor funding, which means they keep more control but have less capital to grow quickly. Lectric's success demonstrates that sometimes the traditional startup path of chasing VC funding isn't the only way to win, and that being lean and focused can outperform well-funded competitors. The e-bike market is competitive globally because electric transportation helps reduce pollution while providing affordable mobility. This story matters to teenagers because it shows that entrepreneurial success has multiple paths—you don't always need to take outside investment to build something meaningful and profitable.

science-space.title: NASA's X-59 Aircraft Flies Supersonic for First Time
science-space.summary: NASA's experimental X-59 aircraft achieved a major breakthrough on June 5 when it successfully flew faster than the speed of sound for the first time. Test pilot Jim "Clue" Less piloted the aircraft from Edwards Air Force Base in California, reaching approximately Mach 1.1 (713 mph)—that's faster than a speeding bullet! The X-59 is special because NASA designed it to create quieter sonic booms, which are the loud explosive sounds produced when aircraft break the sound barrier. Previously, supersonic flight was so loud it disturbed people on the ground, making it impractical for regular travel. This successful flight means we're one step closer to making fast, long-distance airplane travel a reality without annoying everyone below.

science-space.title: Scientists Find Wind Blowing From Our Milky Way's Black Hole After Half-Century Search
science-space.summary: After 50 years of searching, astronomers have finally detected powerful winds blowing from Sagittarius A*, the supermassive black hole at the center of our Milky Way galaxy. A black hole is an area in space with gravity so extreme that nothing—not even light—can escape once it gets too close. Sagittarius A* is millions of times more massive than our Sun and sits about 26,000 light-years away from Earth. Scientists have long theorized that black holes should produce winds, but confirming this took decades of observation with advanced telescopes. This discovery helps us understand how black holes affect their surroundings and how they shape entire galaxies, which is crucial for understanding our cosmic neighborhood.

science-space.title: $1.77 Trillion! SpaceX Is About to Become the 7th-Most Valuable American Company
science-space.summary: SpaceX, the rocket company founded by billionaire Elon Musk, is preparing to go public through an IPO (Initial Public Offering), which means regular people will be able to buy pieces of the company by purchasing stock shares. The company is setting its share price at $135 each, which values the entire company at an incredible $1.77 trillion. To put this in perspective, that would make SpaceX the seventh-most valuable company in America, behind only giants like Apple and Microsoft. SpaceX revolutionized spaceflight by creating reusable rockets that land themselves, dramatically reducing the cost of sending things to space. This IPO is significant because it shows how much investors believe in the company's future plans to colonize Mars, build a global internet network, and make space travel accessible to more people.

science-space.title: NASA Announces Winners of 2026 University Innovation Competition
science-space.summary: NASA announced that Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), one of the world's most prestigious engineering universities, won first place in the 2026 RASC-AL (Revolutionary Aerospace Systems Concepts – Academic Linkage) competition. MIT's winning project, called the Exploration-Class Lunar Integrated Power System, focuses on creating reliable power solutions for future lunar bases where astronauts will live and work on the Moon. This annual competition challenges college students worldwide to solve real problems in aerospace engineering by designing innovative systems and prototypes. The competition is important because it encourages young engineers to think creatively about space exploration while NASA gets fresh ideas from the next generation of scientists. MIT also won second place with a different project about Mars exploration infrastructure, showing the university's dominance in space technology innovation.

science-space.title: Astronauts on International Space Station Take Shelter in SpaceX Dragon as Cosmonauts Try to Fix Air Leak
science-space.summary: Astronauts aboard the International Space Station (ISS), a massive laboratory orbiting 250 miles above Earth where scientists from different countries work together, had to briefly take shelter in a SpaceX Dragon spacecraft on June 5 while Russian cosmonauts (that's what Russian astronauts are called) attempted to repair an air leak on the Russian side of the station. The ISS is a partnership between NASA, Russia's space agency, and several other countries, making it a symbol of international cooperation. An air leak is dangerous in space because the station maintains a specific atmospheric pressure to keep astronauts alive and healthy. The Dragon spacecraft, made by SpaceX, serves as a backup escape vehicle if things go wrong. This incident highlights both the risks of living in space and how critical international teamwork is for maintaining the station.

science-space.title: NASA's Artemis II Moon Mission Research Continues on Earth
science-space.summary: NASA's Artemis II crew safely returned to Earth on April 10 after completing a historic mission flying around the Moon, and scientists are still analyzing all the data they collected during this groundbreaking journey. The Artemis program is NASA's ambitious plan to land humans back on the Moon for the first time since 1972, with Artemis II being a crucial test mission to prepare for future Moon landings. The crew didn't land on the Moon this time—they orbited it to test all the systems and gather information about deep space radiation, the Moon's gravity, and spacecraft performance. The research teams are carefully examining observations and measurements to ensure that future Artemis missions will be safe for astronauts who will actually land on the lunar surface. This data is essential for humanity's next giant leap: establishing a permanent human presence on the Moon.

science-space.title: Astrophotographer Captures Colossal 'Godzilla' Plasma Cloud Stalking the Edge of the Sun
science-space.summary: An astrophotographer captured stunning backyard footage of a massive solar prominence—essentially a giant cloud of superheated plasma (ionized gas) erupting from the Sun's surface. The footage shows streams of coronal rain (hot material) flowing downward along the Sun's magnetic field lines, creating an appearance so dramatic that observers nicknamed it the 'Godzilla' plasma cloud. Plasma is the fourth state of matter (after solid, liquid, and gas) and makes up most of the Sun and stars. Solar prominences are common features on the Sun, but this particular one was unusually large and visually impressive. This discovery matters because understanding solar activity helps scientists predict solar storms that can disrupt power grids and satellites on Earth, affecting everything from your smartphone's GPS to electricity in homes and cities.

science-space.title: Colorful, Chaotic Jupiter
science-space.summary: NASA's Juno spacecraft captured a stunning color-enhanced image of Jupiter's northern hemisphere during its 61st close encounter with the giant planet on May 12, 2024. Citizen scientist Gary Eason created this particular image by downloading raw data from Juno's JunoCam instrument and using digital processing techniques to enhance the colors and clarity, showing the chaotic swirling clouds and atmospheric features in incredible detail. Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system and has turbulent weather patterns including massive storms that have raged for centuries. The Juno spacecraft, which has been orbiting Jupiter since 2016, carries special instruments to study the planet's interior structure, magnetic field, and atmosphere, helping scientists understand how giant planets form and evolve. This image exemplifies how NASA shares its data with the public, allowing enthusiasts worldwide to contribute to scientific discovery.

science-space.title: Venus, Jupiter and Mercury Headline a Stunning Planet Parade Through June
science-space.summary: Through June, Venus, Jupiter, and Mercury are creating an extraordinary celestial spectacle visible from Earth, along with two bright stars and the Moon, forming what astronomers call a 'planet parade.' This alignment occurs when planets appear to line up in the night sky from our perspective on Earth, though they're actually at different distances from us in space. Venus will be the brightest object, appearing as the 'evening star' in the western sky just after sunset, while Jupiter and Mercury will also be visible nearby. Mercury is notoriously difficult to spot normally because it stays close to the Sun, but during this alignment window, it becomes visible to the naked eye. This natural phenomenon offers a fantastic opportunity for teenagers to explore astronomy without any special equipment—just look west after sunset and you'll see one of nature's most beautiful displays, reminding us of our place in the solar system.

science-space.title: NASA Concludes Antenna Mishap Investigation, Releases Report
science-space.summary: NASA has completed its investigation into damage that occurred to the Deep Space Station 14 (DSS-14), a massive 70-meter radio-frequency antenna located at the Goldstone Deep Space Communications Complex near Barstow, California. The agency classified the incident as a Type A mishap based on the total cost of repairs, with Type A being NASA's most serious category for accidents. This antenna is crucial because it's part of NASA's Deep Space Network, a system of giant radio dishes around the world that communicate with spacecraft exploring Mars, Jupiter, and beyond, essentially serving as Earth's connection to distant missions. Without working antennas like DSS-14, NASA couldn't send commands to rovers on Mars or receive data and images back to Earth. The investigation report details what went wrong, helping NASA prevent similar incidents and maintain these irreplaceable communication tools that are essential for all deep space exploration.

world-news.title: Iran war live: US strikes Iranian radar sites; Kuwait comes under attack
world-news.summary: The United States has launched military strikes against Iranian radar installations, marking an escalation in tensions between the two countries that have been hostile since Iran's 1979 revolution. In response, Iran or Iranian-backed forces attacked Kuwait, a small oil-rich nation in the Persian Gulf that hosts American military bases. This conflict matters globally because Iran is a major regional power in the Middle East, and any instability there affects oil prices worldwide—which impacts gas prices and inflation everywhere. The UN reports 1.4 million people in Lebanon need emergency aid due to spillover effects from Israeli attacks on Iranian-backed groups operating there. This demonstrates how regional conflicts can quickly expand and harm innocent civilians across multiple countries.

world-news.title: Putin says there is 'no point' meeting Zelensky over ending Ukraine war
world-news.summary: Vladimir Putin, Russia's president who has ruled for over two decades, has rejected Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky's offer for face-to-face peace talks to end the ongoing war. Zelensky had publicly called for direct negotiations, hoping diplomacy could stop the fighting that has killed hundreds of thousands and displaced millions. Putin's refusal suggests Russia sees no immediate reason to compromise, which means the war will likely continue. This matters because the Ukraine-Russia conflict has destabilized global food and energy markets, affected NATO (a military alliance of Western countries), and could expand if other nations get drawn in. When world leaders refuse to negotiate, it usually means the conflict will worsen before any peace is possible.

world-news.title: 'Pervasive fear' grips Gaza as Israeli attacks persist despite ceasefire
world-news.summary: Despite an announced ceasefire agreement, Israeli military operations continue in Gaza, a Palestinian territory under Israeli military control, leaving residents in constant fear for their safety. A recent drone strike killed a young woman and injured fifteen others near Khan Younis, demonstrating that civilian life remains extremely dangerous. A ceasefire is supposed to be a temporary pause in fighting to allow humanitarian aid and negotiations, but when it's not being honored, it breaks down trust between parties. This matters globally because the Israeli-Palestinian conflict has international implications—it influences Middle Eastern politics, affects UN credibility, and sparks protests worldwide about human rights. When ceasefires fail, it signals that peace agreements are fragile and more violence is likely.

world-news.title: France opens 'war crimes' probe into Israel's treatment of Gaza activists
world-news.summary: France has launched an official investigation into allegations that Israeli forces abused and tortured French activists who were part of a humanitarian aid flotilla (a group of boats carrying supplies) heading to Gaza. War crimes are serious violations of international laws that protect civilians during conflicts, and investigations into them carry significant legal weight in the global community. This is important because it shows how the Israeli-Palestinian conflict affects international relations—France is using its legal system to challenge Israeli military conduct, which could strain France-Israel diplomatic relations. When countries investigate each other's military actions, it can escalate tensions and make future negotiations harder. This reflects growing international scrutiny of how all parties conduct warfare and treat civilians.

world-news.title: Ukraine strikes cargo ships and admits Romania drone blast
world-news.summary: Ukraine has confirmed military strikes against five cargo ships in waters controlled by Russia or near Russian-occupied territories, and admitted responsibility for a drone attack that hit Romanian territory. These actions show Ukraine taking offensive military measures against Russian supply lines and infrastructure. Ukraine is fighting for its survival against Russian invasion, and disrupting cargo shipments (often carrying military supplies or resources) weakens the enemy's ability to wage war. However, the drone blast in Romania—a NATO member country and Ukraine's ally—raises concerns about accidental escalation. When military operations cross borders into allied nations, even accidentally, it risks pulling more countries into the conflict and destabilizing the entire region.

world-news.title: Trump makes pitch to farmers hard-hit by tariffs, high prices in Wisconsin
world-news.summary: Former US President Donald Trump is campaigning in Wisconsin to win back support from rural farmers who have suffered economically from tariffs—taxes added to imported goods that make them more expensive. Trump is claiming these hardships stem from high prices and the economic fallout from conflict with Iran, framing himself as the solution. This matters because farmers are a crucial voting bloc in American elections, and whoever wins their support often wins crucial swing states like Wisconsin. Tariffs are meant to protect domestic industries but often backfire by raising prices for everyday goods and hurting farmers who export crops. Understanding how trade policy affects ordinary people's wallets is essential—when politicians impose tariffs, grocery prices and fuel costs can spike, affecting your family's budget.

world-news.title: Trump hails jobs surge, says Iran talks 'going well'
world-news.summary: Donald Trump has announced positive job growth numbers while simultaneously claiming that negotiations with Iran 'seem to be going quite well,' suggesting diplomatic progress in resolving US-Iran tensions. If true, this would represent a major shift in US foreign policy, as relations between America and Iran have been extremely hostile since Iran's 1979 revolution and the 2015 nuclear agreement collapse. Successful negotiations with Iran could reduce military conflict in the Middle East, stabilize oil markets, and decrease global tension. However, Trump's claims about negotiations require verification, as both sides often make optimistic public statements while serious disagreements persist behind closed doors. This matters because US-Iran relations directly impact global stability, oil prices, and whether conflicts in the Middle East escalate or de-escalate.

world-news.title: Zelenskyy: Putin choosing war by rejecting offer for in-person talks
world-news.summary: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has responded to Putin's refusal to meet by saying the Russian leader is deliberately choosing continued warfare over peaceful resolution. Zelensky's statement frames Putin as responsible for ongoing destruction and loss of life, which is a common diplomatic tactic—publicly blaming the other side for refusing negotiations puts international pressure on them and rallies domestic and international support. This matters because when leaders make public accusations like this, it hardens positions and makes future negotiations harder, as both sides become locked into defending their reputations. The rhetoric reveals that despite desperate need for peace talks, both Russia and Ukraine are far apart on what any agreement would look like. When leaders use war-or-peace language publicly, it signals to the world how little hope remains for quick diplomatic solutions.

world-news.title: Gaza fishermen stay afloat with dinghies made from doorframes
world-news.summary: Fishermen in Gaza are improvising survival by constructing boats from salvaged materials—fiberglass, wood, and doorframes recovered from destroyed buildings—because the fishing industry has been devastated by conflict. This story illustrates the humanitarian crisis in Gaza, where basic economic activities have collapsed due to years of blockade and military operations. Fishing was traditionally a major livelihood for Gaza's population, and the inability to fish safely means families lose income and food sources. This matters globally because it demonstrates how prolonged conflict destroys civilian livelihoods and creates long-term poverty and dependence on international aid. When regions lose their economic capacity to feed themselves, it creates instability, migration, and requires sustained international humanitarian intervention—burdens that affect many countries.

world-news.title: Iran footballers issued US visas for World Cup, says White House
world-news.summary: The White House has announced that Iranian national football team players have received visas to enter the United States for their World Cup match against New Zealand in Los Angeles on June 15, just ten days before the game. This is significant because the US and Iran have had no diplomatic relations and mutual hostility for decades, making even routine visa approvals carry political weight. Allowing Iranian athletes to compete in America symbolizes that despite military tensions, some level of international engagement and normalcy persists through sports diplomacy. Sports can sometimes serve as a bridge between hostile nations—when athletes from enemy countries compete together, it humanizes the other side and reminds people that conflict doesn't define entire populations. This small gesture suggests both nations recognize the value of maintaining some cultural and diplomatic channels even during tensions.

finance-markets.title: Investors boost bets for Fed rate rise after bumper US jobs report
finance-markets.summary: The US economy added 172,000 jobs in May, which sounds great but actually spooked investors. Here's why: the Federal Reserve (America's central bank that controls interest rates) might raise rates if the job market is too strong. When the Fed raises rates, borrowing money becomes more expensive—your parents pay more for mortgages, companies invest less, and stock prices often fall. This jobs report suggested the economy is healthier than expected, making Fed officials more likely to hike rates soon to prevent inflation (when prices of everything go up too fast). Investors are now betting on a rate increase, which is why stock markets dropped and bond yields (returns on government loans) spiked higher.

finance-markets.title: Nasdaq tumbles 4% as shares in chip and memory groups sink
finance-markets.summary: The Nasdaq stock index (which tracks big tech companies) dropped 4%, meaning trillions of dollars in value disappeared in one day. The main reason: rising bond yields, which happen when people think interest rates will go up. When bond yields rise, bonds become more attractive investments than stocks (because they're safer). Tech and chip companies got hit hardest because they're expensive to borrow money for—when rates go up, their future profits are worth less today. The Nasdaq is especially full of tech firms like Apple, Microsoft, and Nvidia (which makes AI chips), so this index suffers more than others when investors get nervous about rate hikes. This selloff is a warning signal that the market's historic winning streak might be ending.

finance-markets.title: SpaceX signs $30bn deal to lease computing capacity to Google
finance-markets.summary: Elon Musk's SpaceX just signed a massive $30 billion deal with Google to provide computing power (essentially renting out computer servers). This is huge because it shows how valuable AI computing capacity has become—companies are willing to pay enormous sums for access to advanced computers. The deal also hints that SpaceX might go public soon through an IPO (initial public offering), which means regular people could buy stock in the company. When SpaceX goes public, it could be the largest IPO ever, attracting millions of retail investors (everyday people trading stocks). This matters because it shows how tech companies are shifting from just building software to controlling the physical infrastructure—the actual hardware and computing power—that powers AI globally.

finance-markets.title: Meta weighs big equity raising after blockbuster Google deal
finance-markets.summary: Meta (Facebook's parent company) is thinking about selling tens of billions of dollars in new stock to raise money for AI infrastructure. This would be called an equity raise—where a company sells new shares to the public, bringing in cash but also diluting existing shareholders' ownership. Meta is considering this partly because of the Google deal with SpaceX, which shows how much companies are willing to spend on AI computing power. Building the data centers and servers needed for AI is incredibly expensive, costing hundreds of billions of dollars industry-wide. By selling new stock, Meta gets the money without taking on debt (which would require interest payments). However, if Meta sells too much new stock, the price per share drops for existing shareholders, which is why this is a big decision.

finance-markets.title: Nasdaq 100 Sinks 5% in AI-Led Rout as Yields Climb: Markets Wrap
finance-markets.summary: Wall Street's winning streak ended with the Nasdaq 100 (a tech-heavy index) dropping 5% in a single day—an AI-led rout means tech stocks crashed hard. This happened because bond yields climbed after the strong jobs report, making investors rethink their strategy. For months, investors poured money into tech and AI stocks, betting those companies would keep growing fast. But when the Federal Reserve signals it might raise interest rates, those expensive growth stocks become less attractive because future earnings are worth less in today's money. The selloff tested whether retail investors (everyday people buying stocks) would panic or hold steady. This matters to teenagers because some of you might be starting to invest, and this shows how quickly markets can reverse—the fastest growing sector one week can crash the next.

finance-markets.title: Trump says US may take equity stakes in AI companies
finance-markets.summary: Trump suggested the US government might buy ownership stakes in AI companies as a way to address voter concerns about AI technology ahead of November's elections. An equity stake means the government would own part of these companies—it's like if your school bought shares in Apple. This is unusual because governments typically don't own pieces of private companies, but Trump frames it as a 'partnership' to help Americans benefit from AI's growth. The real reason: AI is a hot-button political issue. People worry about job losses and whether AI companies are trustworthy, so Trump is trying to show he's keeping America in control of AI development. If this happens, it could reshape how AI companies operate and might set a precedent for government ownership in tech. It also signals that whoever wins November's election will make AI policy a major priority.

finance-markets.title: Raizen Inks $13 Billion Out-of-Court Debt Deal With Creditors
finance-markets.summary: Raizen, a major Brazilian sugar-and-ethanol producer, reached a $13 billion debt restructuring deal with its creditors without going to court. When a company has too much debt and can't pay it back, they can either file for bankruptcy (a legal process) or negotiate with creditors (lenders) to reduce what they owe. Raizen's out-of-court deal is better because it avoids lengthy court battles and lets the company keep operating. Brazil's sugar and ethanol industry is crucial globally—Brazil produces about a third of the world's sugar—so Raizen's financial trouble matters internationally. This deal shows how companies manage debt crises in challenging economic times. If you're studying global economics, understand that emerging markets (developing countries like Brazil) often face currency and debt problems, and restructuring deals like this are how they recover and keep economies functioning.

finance-markets.title: KPMG Chief Economist Discusses Fed Rate Hike Expectations
finance-markets.summary: Diane Swonk, a top economist at KPMG (one of the world's largest accounting firms), explained that the Federal Reserve is likely to raise interest rates because the job market is strong but inflation (rising prices) is still hanging around, especially in services like restaurants and haircuts. Bond markets are currently pricing in a 25 basis point rate hike by 2026 (a basis point is 0.01%, so 25 basis points equals 0.25%). This is what economists call 'hawkish sentiment'—basically, Fed officials are getting tougher on inflation and willing to slow the economy to fight it. For teenagers, this matters because rate hikes eventually increase credit card rates, student loan rates, and reduce returns on savings accounts. Understanding what economists like Swonk are saying helps you anticipate whether it's a good time to borrow money or save.

finance-markets.title: US demands tougher rules for car parts in Mexico trade talks
finance-markets.summary: The US is pushing for stricter rules requiring more American-made parts in North American cars during trade negotiations with Mexico. The goal is to reduce how many Chinese parts end up in vehicles made in Mexico or North America—this is called 'reshoring' when countries try to bring manufacturing back home. Tariffs (taxes on imports) and rules like these shape where companies build things, which affects where jobs go. For example, if the US makes it expensive or complicated to use Chinese parts, automakers might choose American suppliers instead, creating US manufacturing jobs. This matters because trade policy directly impacts whether your country's economy grows or shrinks, how many jobs exist, and whether goods cost more. Understanding trade conflicts helps explain why your car, phone, or clothes cost what they do and why politicians care so much about 'Made in America.'

finance-markets.title: Tech Selloff, Bitcoin Drop Test Retail Investor Strength Ahead of SpaceX IPO
finance-markets.summary: Friday's tech stock crash and cryptocurrency (like Bitcoin) drop tested whether retail investors—everyday people trading stocks through apps like Robinhood—would panic sell or stay confident before SpaceX's massive upcoming IPO. For years, retail investors have been a reliable force propping up stock prices, willing to buy almost anything, especially hot tech stocks and crypto. But when markets crash hard, these traders sometimes panic and sell everything, which can make crashes worse. The SpaceX IPO is expected to attract millions of retail traders hoping to get rich quick. This matters because it shows how retail investors increasingly move markets, and it highlights the risk of overconfidence—when people think they know more than they actually do. If SpaceX IPO prices too high and crashes afterward, many retail investors could lose serious money, teaching a harsh lesson about investing without understanding what you're buying.

Solar power has been a major element of China’s renewables buildout since the mid-2010s.  The...

Carbon dioxide removal (CDR) technologies will need to be deployed at rates even faster than...

energy-climate.title: Chinese PV Industry Brief: Arctech wins 2.1 GW solar deal
energy-climate.summary:
Arctech has secured supply agreements tied to a 2.1 GW solar project in the United Arab Emirates, while China Energy Engineering Corp. has launched a 20 GW photovoltaic inverter procurement tender.

asia-pacific.title: Does Pete Hegseth’s volte-face on China reflect an America in decline?
asia-pacific.summary:
Listening to US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth at the recently concluded Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore, I thought of the iconic Sichuan opera act of face-changing, where performers switch masks in an instant.
Last year, Hegseth’s speech at the event was filled with blatant attacks on China. This year, he put on a completely different face, declaring that: “Under President Trump’s leadership, relations between the United States and China are better than they’ve been in many years.”

asia-pacific.title: How everyday cooking oil is helping Japan fight an aviation fuel crisis
asia-pacific.summary:
Japanese homemaker Maki Watanabe carefully ⁠pours into a plastic bottle the oil she used to cook her ⁠deep-fried aubergines, doing her part in her Tokyo kitchen for a national effort to ramp up production of eco-friendly jet fuel.
“It would take a tremendous amount to make an aircraft fly, so I hope we can collect more,” said Watanabe, whose penchant for cooking allows her to donate about 40 litres (10 gallons) a year.
Her contribution is pooled at a nearby supermarket that is among roughly 300.

asia-pacific.title: Don’t dream it’s over: mainland students rethink Hong Kong over costs, culture
asia-pacific.summary:
Weeks before mainland Chinese student Carol Chen graduated from Baptist University in Hong Kong in July, the 22-year-old did the math and weighed the cost of living in the city against returning home.
Renting a room in the city would consume around half of the HK$20,000 (US$2,552) starting salary she expected as a junior data analyst.
“If I go back to Shanghai, I will only need to worry about daily expenses,” said Chen, a maths and statistics graduate.

The incident unfolded on the morning of May 27 in a nursery in Fengxian county, Jiangsu province, eastern China.

“Those errors can be reprogrammed. It becomes possible to use AI to read, rewrite and reverse cells, or at least slow the ageing process,” Lai said in an interview with the South China Morning Post.

Waves break along Cenang Beach as the sun drops over the Andaman Sea, washing Langkawi’s white sand in gold. Tourists sip fresh coconuts beneath rows of bright umbrellas, gazing out at the “Jewel of Kedah”: a duty-free archipelago long sold as one of Malaysia’s premier tropical escapes.
Out on the water, meanwhile, small boats traverse the 8km (five miles) of open water that separates Langkawi from Thailand’s Koh Tarutao. On fast boats, the crossing takes a matter of minutes. No navigation...

asia-pacific.title: Putin says no reason to meet Ukraine’s Zelensky, calls his letter ‘rude’
asia-pacific.summary:
Russian President Vladimir Putin said on Friday he currently saw no reason to meet Volodymyr Zelensky after the Ukrainian president published an open letter proposing they hold face-to-face talks to agree an end to a war now in its fifth year.
In his letter, which was sent to other countries, including the US, Zelensky said most Russians ‌had grown tired of Ukrainian missile and drone attacks, high inflation and fuel shortages, and were ready for peace.
